questions.question: In the recursive visualization of fib(5), why do multiple nodes with the same label (e.g., f(2)) appear?

answers.a: Because Fibonacci numbers repeat in the sequence
answers.b: Because the recursive function recomputes the same subproblems
answers.c: Because the visualization duplicates nodes for clarity
answers.d: Because f(2) is a base case

explanations.a: Repeating values do not automatically cause repeated calls.
explanations.b: Recursive Fibonacci recalculates the same values, which appears as duplicate nodes.
explanations.c: The visualization reflects real execution, not duplication.
explanations.d: f(2) is not a base case.

questions.question: When stepping through the recursive simulation, why does the call stack grow before any values are returned?

answers.b: Because recursion must reach base cases before returning
answers.c: Because Fibonacci values are stored later

explanations.a: Return statements execute after recursive calls.
explanations.b: Recursive calls keep stacking until a base case is reached.
explanations.c: Values are computed through returns, not storage.
explanations.d: This behavior is intrinsic to recursion.

questions.question: In the iterative Fibonacci simulation, how does the execution flow differ from the recursive version?

answers.a: It branches into two paths at each step
answers.b: It uses a single linear sequence of updates
answers.c: It repeatedly calls the same function
answers.d: It creates a call tree internally

explanations.b: Iterative Fibonacci updates values step by step in a loop.
explanations.c: Iteration does not involve function calls.
explanations.d: Iterative execution has no call tree.

questions.question: Based on the simulation, which visualization shows a call tree structure?

explanations.b: Iterative Fibonacci follows a linear loop.
explanations.c: Recursive Fibonacci naturally forms a branching call tree.

questions.question: Why does the total number of calls grow rapidly in the recursive simulation as n increases?

answers.a: Each function call creates two new calls except at base cases

explanations.a: Each non-base call branches into two recursive calls.
explanations.b: Stack depth grows linearly, not exponentially.
explanations.c: Loops are used in iteration, not recursion.

questions.question: When comparing both simulations, which approach uses constant extra memory?

explanations.b: Iterative Fibonacci uses only a few variables.

questions.question: While observing both modes, why does the iterative simulation complete with far fewer steps?

explanations.a: Base cases are implicit in iteration.
explanations.b: Iterative Fibonacci computes each value exactly once.
explanations.d: Returns are not applicable in loops.

questions.question: If memoization were added to the recursive simulation, how would it compare visually to the iterative version?

answers.a: It would still show a full branching tree
answers.b: It would look closer to a linear flow

explanations.b: Eliminating repeated calls makes execution closer to iterative behavior.

questions.question: After using both simulations, which statement best summarizes the trade-off?

answers.b: Iterative is faster but harder to understand
answers.c: Recursive is intuitive but inefficient, iterative is efficient but less visual

explanations.c: This matches what the simulations demonstrate.
